titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the John Taylor Correspondence
titlestmt.author: by Beinecke Staff

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Friday November 6, 2020 at 05:43

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

did.abstract: The collection consists of letters addressed to Taylor by various correspondents, including Richard Ayton, Octavius Gilchrist, Alexander Haden and Harvey Marriott. Most concern publishing business or personal news; several discuss currency policy, an issue of great interest to Taylor.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Bequest of James Marshall and Marie-Louise Osborn. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The John Taylor Correspondence is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: John Taylor Correspondence. James Marshall and Marie-Louise Osborn Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: The materials are open for research.

bioghist.head: JOHN TAYLOR, 1781-1864
bioghist.p: John Taylor was born in Nottinghamshire in 1781. Taylor moved to London about 1806, becoming a partner in the publishing firm Taylor & Hussey (later Taylor & Walton). Taylor published several works attributing the letters of "Junius" to Sir Philip Francis, an identification still widely accepted today, and served as editor of the London Magazine in 1821-24. Taylor's literary friends included Charles Lamb, Thomas Hood, and Samuel Coleridge.

Taylor, perhaps influenced by his brother, the banker James Taylor (1788-1863), was opposed to the currency policies of Sir Thomas Peel, and authored several books and pamphlets on the subject of currency reform, including "Currency Fallacies Refuted and Paper Money Vindicated" (London, 1833) and "The Monetary Policy of England and America" (London, 1843). His other works include "The Great Pyramid: Why Was It Built?" (London, 1859), and articles on antiquarian subjects for Macmillan's and the Gentleman's Magazine.
John Taylor did not marry. He died in Kensington on July 5, 1864.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The John Taylor Correspondence consists of letters sent to John Taylor, publisher. The collection spans the dates 1803-1861.

The majority of the letters concern publication business, particularly requests for payments and advances, and some contain personal news as well. Correspondents include the Revs. Basil and Harvey Marriott; Alexander Bunn Haden; Charles Caleb Colton; and the topographical writer Richard Ayton, whose letters date from the last year of his life and cover literary and political opinions as well as the gradual decline of his health. The letters of Octavius Gilchrist discuss progress on his edition of the poetry of Richard Corbet, as well as other literary projects.
Other correspondents, including John Knott and John and Philip Twells, share Taylor's interest in currency reform and offer news of the Currency Society.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Correspondence is alphabetically arranged by name of correspondent.

did.unittitle: 4 ALS to John Taylor; East Retford

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Accompanied by a letter to Miss White.

did.unittitle: 17 ALS to Taylor and Hessey; Seaford and London
5 ALS to Taylor and Hessey; Tiverton

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On the publication of his writings. Accompanied by a copy of a letter from Taylor and Hessey to Colton, 1813 Dec 12.

did.unittitle: 8 ALS to John Taylor; Camden Town and So. Carlton
3 ALS to John Taylor; South Carlton
ALS to John Taylor; South Carlton

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: On Gilchrist's edition of Richard Corbet's poetry and other literary enterprises.

did.unittitle: ALS to John Taylor; Chapel Ash
2 ALS to John Taylor; Balham and Horsham
9 ALS to John Taylor; East Retford
ALS to an unnamed recipient, Retford
4 ALS to John Taylor; 1 Park St., Edinburgh

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: With a fragment by George Mower on the finding of a giant skeleton.

did.unittitle: 2 ALS to John Taylor; York St. and Worcester
ALS (copy) to Philip Twells; Leonard Place

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Concerning the Currency Society, death of Lord George Bentinck, etc.

did.unittitle: 3 ALS to John Taylor; Lincolns Inn

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Social and business notes. On p. 4 of one letter, some apparently unrelated genealogical notes in another hand.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: One letter written on p. 4 of a letter written by his son, F. White, Jr.

did.unittitle: 2 ALS to John Taylor; Portsmouth and Cawsand
3 ALS to John Taylor; East Retford
